root_cause.text: An integer overflow occurs in the `EVP_EncodeUpdate` function when adding the input length (`inl`) to the existing buffered data length (`ctx->num`), which allows maliciously large inputs to bypass a buffer length check and cause heap memory corruption via `memcpy`.
root_cause.evidence: The patch changes the vulnerable bounds check `if ((ctx->num + inl) < ctx->length)` to `if (ctx->length - ctx->num > inl)`, which effectively prevents the addition operation from overflowing.

agent_logs.root_cause_candidate: An integer overflow occurs in the `EVP_EncodeUpdate` function when adding the input length (`inl`) to the existing buffered data length (`ctx->num`), which allows maliciously large inputs to bypass a buffer length check and cause heap memory corruption via `memcpy`.
agent_logs.evidence_points: The patch changes the vulnerable bounds check `if ((ctx->num + inl) < ctx->length)` to `if (ctx->length - ctx->num > inl)`, which effectively prevents the addition operation from overflowing.

intent_record.intent_summary: Fix potential integer overflow in the bounds check by rearranging the arithmetic comparison.
intent_record.what: Rewrote the arithmetic condition to use subtraction instead of addition.
intent_record.how: Prevents potential integer overflow by avoiding the addition of the input length and current number of bytes before comparing against the maximum length.

grouping_result.intent: Fix potential integer overflow in the bounds check by rearranging the arithmetic comparison.

relevance_review.reason: The group's changes directly address the integer overflow in the bounds check, which is the exact root cause of the vulnerability.

candidates.rationale: This line performs the addition (ctx->num + inl) before checking against ctx->length, which is susceptible to integer overflow and allows maliciously large inputs to bypass the bounds check.

vuln_statements.explanation: This statement contains the vulnerable arithmetic operation (ctx->num + inl) which can overflow, allowing maliciously large inputs to bypass the bounds check and lead to heap memory corruption.

--- a/crypto/evp/encode.c
+++ b/crypto/evp/encode.c
@@ -5,21 +5,21 @@
  * This package is an SSL implementation written
  * by Eric Young (eay@cryptsoft.com).
  * The implementation was
bic_trace_step.reason: The function `EVP_EncodeUpdate` contains the vulnerable logic: `if ((ctx->num + inl) < ctx->length) { memcpy(&(ctx->enc_data[ctx->num]), in, inl); ... }`. There is no check to prevent integer overflow when adding `ctx->num` and `inl`, which can bypass the length check and result in a heap memory corruption via `memcpy`. The commit only changes formatting.

bic_trace_step.commit_msg: Import of old SSLeay release: SSLeay 0.8.1b
bic_trace_step.code_snippet:
commit d02b48c63a58ea4367a0e905979f140b7d090f86
Author: Ralf S. Engelschall <rse@openssl.org>
Date:   Mon Dec 21 10:52:47 1998 +0000

    Import of old SSLeay release: SSLeay 0.8.1b

diff --git a/crypto/evp/encode.c b/crypto/evp/encode.c
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000..7cd65244e6
--- /dev/null
+++ b/crypto/evp/encode.c
@@ -0,0 +1,422 @@
+/* crypto/evp/encode.c */
+/* Copyright (C) 1995-1997 Eric Young (eay@cryptsoft.com)
+ * All rights reserved.
+ *
+ * This package is an SSL implementati
bic_trace_step.reason: The function `EVP_EncodeUpdate` contains the vulnerable construct `if ((ctx->num+inl) < ctx->length)`. Since `ctx->num` and `inl` are signed integers, a sufficiently large `inl` will cause `ctx->num+inl` to overflow, resulting in a value less than `ctx->length`. This allows the execution to reach `memcpy(&(ctx->enc_data[ctx->num]),in,inl);` with a maliciously large `inl`, causing a heap buffer overflow.
